title: GPT and the Generative Line
subtitle: How the other half of the Transformer learned to talk — one word at a time
theme: Generation over comprehension: the decoder-only lineage that captured the world's imagination.
learningObjectives: Explain the decoder-only architecture and autoregressive (next-token) generation.
Trace the GPT line from GPT-1 (2018) to GPT-2 (2019) and what each proved.
Understand why predicting the next word is a deceptively powerful training objective.
Retell the 'too dangerous to release' GPT-2 controversy and what it revealed about AI's social stakes.
Clearly contrast encoder-only, decoder-only, and encoder-decoder architectures and when to use each.
Explain why generation, not understanding, captured public imagination and set up the LLM era.
storyHook: In February 2019, OpenAI did something no AI lab had done before: they announced a model and then refused to fully release it, calling it too dangerous. The model, GPT-2, could write news articles, stories, and arguments so fluent that its creators feared a flood of machine-made misinformation. The world had met a machine that didn't just understand language — it generated it. And nothing would be the same.

slides.title: The Other Branch of the Tree
slides.body:
## Yesterday we read. Today we write.

Yesterday we walked the **encoder** branch — BERT, the silent reader who studies a whole sentence at once to *understand* it. Today we cross to the other branch born from the same 2017 Transformer: the **decoder**, the machine that learned to *generate*.

```
          The Transformer (2017)
          /                    \
   ENCODER half            DECODER half
        |                       |
      BERT (2018)            GPT (2018)
   reads both ways         writes left-to-right
   UNDERSTANDS             GENERATES
```

The acronym tells the story: **GPT = Generative Pre-trained Transformer.** Three words, three ideas:
- **Generative** — it produces new text, not just judgments about existing text.
- **Pre-trained** — like BERT, it learns from a mountain of raw text first.
- **Transformer** — built from the decoder half of the 2017 architecture.

Here's the deep irony of this whole course: BERT was the bigger immediate technical splash in 2018, smashing benchmarks and quietly powering Google. GPT was quieter at first. But it was the *generative* line that would eventually explode into ChatGPT and reshape the world. Today we learn why generation turned out to be the path to general intelligence — and why it scared its own creators.
slides.speakerNotes: Open by reconnecting to yesterday — physically point back at the fork diagram. The key emotional hook: 'The model that won the headlines in 2018 (BERT) is NOT the one that changed the world. The quiet sibling did.' Ask the class: 'Why do you think a machine that TALKS captures imagination more than one that READS?' Let them feel that generation is inherently more visible, more magical, more frightening.
slides.marketConnection: As of June 2026, the entire consumer AI boom — ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and every chatbot — descends from this decoder-only line; generation is the face of AI to the public.

slides.title: Autoregression: Predicting One Word at a Time
slides.body:
## The deceptively simple core idea

A decoder-only model does exactly one thing, over and over: **given all the words so far, predict the next word.** Then it adds that word to the sequence and predicts again. And again. This is called **autoregressive generation**.

```
Prompt:  'The weather today is'

step 1:  The weather today is -> [sunny]   (pick a word)
step 2:  The weather today is sunny -> [and]
step 3:  The weather today is sunny and -> [warm]
step 4:  ... and warm -> [.]

Result:  'The weather today is sunny and warm.'
```

The model outputs a **probability distribution** over its entire vocabulary at every step — 'sunny' 40%, 'cold' 15%, 'nice' 10%, and so on — and a sampling strategy picks one. Loop until done.

### Why left-to-right, and why 'masked' attention
Unlike BERT, a decoder is **not** allowed to peek ahead. When predicting word 5, it can only see words 1-4. This is enforced by **causal (masked) self-attention** — the model is blindfolded to the future. This constraint is essential: at generation time there *is* no future yet — the model is creating it. You can only predict the next word using the past.

```
BERT  (bidirectional):  <- word -> can see both sides
GPT   (causal):         word -> | can only see the past
                              (future is blocked)
```

That single design choice — blindfold the future — is the entire difference in how the two branches read text.
slides.speakerNotes: Autoregression is THE concept of the day — make it physical. Do a live 'human autoregression': you say three words, point at a student to add the next plausible word, then the next student, around the room. Watch a sentence emerge from pure local prediction. Then drop the insight: 'Notice you each only saw the past, never the future, and yet a coherent sentence formed.' That's GPT. The causal mask is just 'no peeking ahead'.
slides.marketConnection: Every token you watch stream onto your screen from ChatGPT or Claude in 2026 is one step of this exact autoregressive loop — the typewriter effect IS next-token prediction happening live.

slides.title: Why 'Predict the Next Word' Is Secretly Profound
slides.body:
## A trivial-sounding task that demands everything

When people first hear 'GPT just predicts the next word', they shrug — it sounds like glorified autocomplete. The profound realization is this: **to predict the next word really well, you must understand almost everything.**

Consider what it takes to correctly complete these:

```
'The Eiffel Tower is located in the city of ____'   -> needs FACTS (Paris)
'2 plus 2 equals ____'                              -> needs ARITHMETIC (4)
'She was furious, her voice trembling with ____'    -> needs EMOTION (rage)
'def add(a, b): return a ____'                       -> needs CODE (+ b)
'The opposite of hot is ____'                        -> needs SEMANTICS (cold)
'Q: Capital of Japan? A: ____'                       -> needs Q&A REASONING (Tokyo)
```

To nail the next word across *all of human text*, a model is forced to implicitly learn grammar, world facts, arithmetic, logic, sentiment, translation, coding conventions, and the structure of arguments. Next-word prediction isn't a narrow task — it's a *universal* one that happens to require general competence as a side effect.

### The compression view
Another way to see it: to predict text well, the model must build a compressed internal model of the world that produced that text. Great prediction *requires* understanding. The 'autocomplete' framing is technically true and deeply misleading — it's like calling a novelist 'someone who decides what letter comes next'.

This is the seed of everything. The bet of the GPT line is: **scale up next-word prediction far enough, and general intelligence emerges.** Tomorrow's class (GPT-3 and scaling laws) is the story of that bet paying off spectacularly.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the intellectual heart of the entire course — give it room. Run the examples on the board and have the class fill each blank, then point out the DIFFERENT skill each one required. The aha moment: one objective (next word) secretly demands ALL skills. Use the novelist analogy. Then plant the seed for tomorrow: 'Hold onto this idea — that a dumb-sounding objective hides a universal one. Tomorrow we find out what happens when you make it 1000x bigger.'
slides.marketConnection: This insight — that next-token prediction at scale yields general capability — is the foundational thesis of every frontier lab in 2026; the entire industry is a bet on this one idea.

slides.title: GPT-1 (2018): The Quiet First Step
slides.body:
## The proof of concept that nobody noticed

In June 2018 — months *before* BERT — OpenAI published 'Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training'. This was **GPT-1**: 117 million parameters, 12 decoder layers, trained on BooksCorpus (~7,000 unpublished books).

### What GPT-1 proved
GPT-1's contribution was the *recipe*, not the size:
1. **Generative pre-training** on raw text using next-word prediction (unsupervised).
2. **Discriminative fine-tuning** on specific tasks afterward.

Notice this is the same two-stage idea BERT would popularize months later — pretrain on raw text, then fine-tune. GPT-1 got there first, but with a left-to-right generative objective instead of masking. It showed that a single pre-trained model could be fine-tuned to do many different language tasks (entailment, similarity, question answering) and do them well.

### Why it stayed quiet
GPT-1 was a solid result, but a few months later BERT arrived, was bidirectional, and beat it on the understanding benchmarks everyone cared about. The NLP community's attention swung to encoders. GPT-1 looked like the runner-up.

But OpenAI had a different bet. They didn't think the answer was a cleverer objective. They thought the answer was **more** — more data, more parameters, more compute, all pointed at pure generation. GPT-1 was step one of a deliberate scaling ladder. They were about to climb the next rung, and it would shock everyone.
slides.speakerNotes: Frame GPT-1 as the underdog and the quiet conviction behind it. The key narrative beat: OpenAI looked like they'd lost to BERT in 2018, but they were playing a longer game — a scaling bet, not an architecture bet. Ask the class: 'When everyone zigs (toward clever bidirectional designs), what does it take to keep zagging (just make it bigger)?' This sets up the theme of conviction-under-doubt that defines the GPT story.
slides.marketConnection: The 'scaling over cleverness' bet that OpenAI made in 2018 became the dominant industrial strategy by 2026 — though, as we'll see next week, the field is now actively debating where that bet stops paying off.

slides.title: GPT-2 (2019): The Model That Could Write
slides.body:
## 10x bigger, and suddenly it had things to say

In February 2019, OpenAI unveiled **GPT-2**: same decoder-only design, but scaled up dramatically — the largest version had **1.5 billion parameters** (over 10x GPT-1) and trained on **WebText**, ~8 million web pages curated from outbound Reddit links with decent karma (a filter for 'humans found this worth sharing').

### What changed with scale
GPT-2 didn't just do tasks better — it could *write*. Given a prompt, it produced multiple paragraphs of coherent, on-topic, stylistically consistent text. The famous demo: prompted with a fake news lede about **unicorns discovered in the Andes speaking perfect English**, GPT-2 continued with a straight-faced, journalistically-styled article complete with invented quotes from a fictional scientist. It was fluent, confident, and entirely fabricated.

### Zero-shot surprises
GPT-2 also showed early signs of doing tasks **it was never explicitly trained for**, just by being prompted. Add 'TL;DR:' after an article and it would attempt a summary. Give it 'English: ... French: ...' patterns and it would attempt translation. No fine-tuning — just prompting. This was the first real glimpse of what we'd later call **in-context learning** (tomorrow's big theme), and it hinted that a big enough generative model is a kind of general-purpose engine.

### The quality jump
The leap from GPT-1 to GPT-2 wasn't a new idea — it was the *same* idea, made bigger. And the result was qualitatively different. That observation — 'we just scaled it and new abilities appeared' — would become the defining pattern of the next several years.
slides.speakerNotes: The unicorn story is a crowd-pleaser — describe it vividly and note the eerie part: it INVENTED a scientist and a quote. That's both the magic and the danger. Emphasize the 'TL;DR:' trick hard — it's the first whiff of in-context learning, which is tomorrow's headline. The repeated drumbeat: 'same idea, just bigger, and something new appeared.' Ask: 'If making it 10x bigger gave it the ability to write, what might 100x give it?' (Tomorrow's answer: a lot.)
slides.marketConnection: GPT-2's 'TL;DR' and translation-by-prompting moments were the embryonic form of the prompting-based workflows that the entire 2026 AI industry now runs on — no fine-tuning, just instructions.

slides.title: 'Too Dangerous to Release': The GPT-2 Drama
slides.body:
## The day an AI lab got scared of its own creation

When OpenAI announced GPT-2 in February 2019, they did something unprecedented: **they refused to release the full model.** Citing fears of misuse — mass-produced fake news, spam, impersonation, propaganda — they published only a small version and a paper, holding back the 1.5B-parameter model.

### The reaction split the field
- **Praise:** Some hailed it as responsible disclosure — finally a lab taking the social risks of AI seriously before, not after, harm.
- **Criticism:** Others called it a publicity stunt, or argued that withholding the model hurt open science and safety research (you can't study what you can't access), while not actually stopping determined bad actors who could retrain it.

### The staged release
OpenAI navigated the controversy with a **staged rollout**: 124M parameters first, then 355M, then 774M, and finally — in November 2019, nine months later — the full **1.5B** model. By then, the predicted apocalypse of machine-generated misinformation hadn't materialized (yet), and the staged release became a template others would study.

### Why this moment matters in the story
GPT-2 was the moment AI capability and AI *safety* collided in public for the first time at this scale. It forced a permanent question into the field: **just because we CAN build and release something, SHOULD we?** This tension — capability racing ahead of our ability to govern it — becomes a central theme of this entire course. The alignment and safety chapters we'll reach later all trace a line back to this nervous February.
slides.speakerNotes: Tell this as genuine drama — there's no clean hero. Present BOTH sides fairly and ask the class to vote: 'Responsible caution, or publicity stunt?' Get a real debate going; there's no settled answer. Then elevate it: this is the first time 'should we?' beat 'can we?' in public. Connect forward: every safety/alignment debate they'll hear about in 2026 (open vs closed models, release policies) echoes this exact moment.
slides.marketConnection: The 'open vs. closed model' and 'responsible release' debates are still raging in June 2026 — open-weight labs versus closed frontier labs — and GPT-2's staged release was the opening shot of that war.

slides.title: Three Architectures, Three Jobs
slides.body:
## The full family, finally side by side

Now that we've met both branches, let's nail the distinction once and for all. The 2017 Transformer can be used three ways:

```
+-------------------+--------------------+----------------------+
|  ENCODER-ONLY     |  DECODER-ONLY      |  ENCODER-DECODER     |
|  (BERT family)    |  (GPT family)      |  (T5, BART, orig.)   |
+-------------------+--------------------+----------------------+
| reads ALL at once |  generates L-to-R  | reads input fully,   |
| bidirectional     |  causal / masked   | then generates out   |
| UNDERSTANDS       |  GENERATES         | TRANSFORMS A->B      |
+-------------------+--------------------+----------------------+
| classify, NER,    |  chat, write,      | translation,         |
| search, embed,    |  code, complete,   | summarization,       |
| rerank            |  reason            | rewriting            |
+-------------------+--------------------+----------------------+
```

### Encoder-only (BERT)
Reads the whole input bidirectionally and outputs a *representation* or a *judgment*. No generation. Best for understanding tasks.

### Decoder-only (GPT)
Reads left-to-right and *generates* the continuation token by token. Best for open-ended generation. This is the line that became ChatGPT, Claude, and friends.

### Encoder-decoder (the original 2017 design, plus T5 and BART)
Uses an encoder to fully read an input, then a decoder to generate an output — ideal when input and output are *different* sequences, like translating English to French or summarizing a long doc. The encoder understands the source; the decoder writes the target.

### The key intuition
- One sequence in, a *label* out -> **encoder**.
- A prompt in, more text out (same 'stream') -> **decoder**.
- One sequence in, a *different* sequence out -> **encoder-decoder**.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the slide students will photograph — make sure the table is crystal clear. Drill the three-line intuition at the bottom; it's the practical decision rule. Pose three mini-scenarios and have them call out the architecture: 'Detect toxic comments?' (encoder) 'Write a marketing email from a brief?' (decoder) 'Translate a contract to Spanish?' (encoder-decoder, classically). Note that in 2026, giant decoders can do all three by brute force — but knowing the 'right tool' still matters for cost.
slides.marketConnection: By 2026 the decoder-only design has largely won the spotlight (most frontier chat models are decoder-only), but encoder-decoder models still excel at translation and structured transformation, and encoders still own retrieval.

slides.title: Why Generation Captured the Imagination
slides.body:
## Comprehension is useful; generation feels alive

BERT was, by 2018 benchmarks, arguably the bigger technical achievement. So why did the *generative* line become the cultural and commercial phenomenon? Several reasons, and they're worth understanding because they explain the shape of the whole industry.

### 1. Generation is visible and interactive
When BERT improves your search results, you never see the model — it's invisible infrastructure. When GPT writes you a poem, a story, or an answer, you watch a machine *create* in real time. It feels like a conversation with a mind. Humans are wired to be captivated by something that talks back.

### 2. Generation is open-ended and general
An encoder does a *bounded* task (classify into N categories). A generator can, in principle, produce *anything* expressible in language — code, jokes, contracts, sonnets, business plans. Open-endedness reads as creativity, and creativity reads as intelligence.

### 3. Generation is a universal interface
You don't need a separate fine-tuned model per task. With a generative model you just *ask* in plain language. One model, infinite tasks, accessed through conversation. That generality is what made it a product anyone could use, not just an engineer.

### 4. Generation surfaces the 'wow'
A wrong classification is boring. A fluent, surprising, human-like paragraph is uncanny — it triggers an emotional response. The 'wow' drove adoption, investment, fear, and hype in a way benchmark numbers never could.

### The strategic lesson
The generative bet wasn't obviously the winner in 2018. It won because it pointed at *generality* and *interaction* — the two things that turn a research result into a world-changing product. Tomorrow we see what happened when OpenAI poured unprecedented scale into exactly this bet.
slides.speakerNotes: This slide answers a question students genuinely puzzle over: 'If BERT was so good, why didn't IT become ChatGPT?' Hit the four reasons but linger on #3 (universal interface) — that's the real commercial unlock. Ask the class: 'What's the difference between a tool you configure and a tool you just TALK to?' The second one your grandmother can use. Generality + conversation = mass adoption. That's the whole game.
slides.marketConnection: The 'one model, any task, just ask it' paradigm is the entire 2026 AI product economy — the chat interface became the universal front door to computing, and it all started on this generative branch.

slides.title: Hands-On: Watching Autoregression Work
slides.body:
## See next-token prediction with your own eyes

Let's make autoregression concrete. Here's the shape of generating from a small GPT-2 (Hugging Face style):

```python
from transformers import pipeline

gen = pipeline('text-generation', model='gpt2')

out = gen('In the year 2026, artificial intelligence will',
          max_new_tokens=40,
          do_sample=True,        # sample from the distribution
          temperature=0.8)       # creativity dial
print(out[0]['generated_text'])
```

### The two dials that control 'personality'
Generation isn't deterministic — you choose *how* to pick from the probability distribution at each step:

```
temperature = 0.2  -> conservative, repetitive, 'safe' (picks likely words)
temperature = 1.0  -> balanced, natural
temperature = 1.5  -> wild, creative, sometimes incoherent

top_p / top_k      -> only sample from the most probable words,
                      trimming the long tail of weird options
```

### See the distribution itself
The deeper exercise: at a single step, inspect the model's probabilities.

```
Prompt: 'The capital of France is'
  paris  -> 0.62
  the    -> 0.08
  a      -> 0.05
  ...
```

The model isn't 'looking up' Paris — it's computing that 'paris' is the most probable next token given everything it learned. Generation is just doing this, picking one, appending, and repeating.

### The realization
GPT-2 in your hands feels primitive next to 2026 models, and that's the point: you can *feel* the raw mechanism — sometimes brilliant, sometimes drifting into nonsense. Everything since is this same loop, scaled and aligned.
slides.speakerNotes: If you can, run gpt2 live and crank temperature from 0.2 to 1.5 on the same prompt — the class will SEE personality emerge from one number. Then show a probability readout for 'The capital of France is' so they viscerally get 'it's just picking the most likely next token'. Emphasize: GPT-2 will sometimes produce garbage — that's honest. The polish of 2026 models comes from scale (tomorrow) and alignment (next week), not a different core mechanism.
slides.marketConnection: The temperature and top-p dials you set here are the exact same parameters exposed in every 2026 LLM API — when developers tune chatbot 'creativity', they're turning these same knobs on a much bigger version of this loop.

slides.title: Recap: The Generative Branch
slides.body:
## What we locked in today

### The architecture
The **decoder-only** branch of the Transformer. GPT = **G**enerative **P**re-trained **T**ransformer. It generates text **autoregressively** — predict the next token, append, repeat — using **causal (masked) attention** that blindfolds the future.

### The profound objective
'Predict the next word' sounds trivial but secretly demands everything: facts, arithmetic, logic, emotion, code, reasoning. Great prediction *requires* understanding. The GPT bet: scale this up far enough and general intelligence emerges.

### The lineage so far
- **GPT-1 (2018)**: 117M params. Proved generative pretrain + fine-tune. Quiet runner-up to BERT.
- **GPT-2 (2019)**: 1.5B params, 10x bigger. Could *write* (the unicorn article). First glimpses of in-context learning ('TL;DR:'). Same idea, just bigger — and new abilities appeared.

### The drama
GPT-2 was deemed **'too dangerous to release'**, then rolled out in stages. The first public collision of capability and safety — the seed of every alignment debate to come.

### The three architectures
- **Encoder-only (BERT)**: understands. Classify, search, embed.
- **Decoder-only (GPT)**: generates. Chat, write, code.
- **Encoder-decoder (T5/BART)**: transforms A->B. Translate, summarize.

### Why generation won the spotlight
Visible, interactive, open-ended, and accessed through plain conversation — a universal interface that turned a research result into a world-changing product.

```
The bet: scale next-word prediction -> general intelligence.
Tomorrow: what happened when they REALLY scaled it.
```
slides.speakerNotes: Keep this brisk and confident — you're consolidating, not teaching new ideas. Land the three pillars: autoregression, the profundity of next-word prediction, and the encoder/decoder/encoder-decoder map. End on the cliffhanger: 'GPT-2 was 1.5 billion parameters and people were already amazed and scared. Tomorrow: GPT-3, ONE HUNDRED AND SEVENTY-FIVE BILLION. And it did something nobody predicted — it learned new tasks from examples in the prompt, with no training at all.'
slides.marketConnection: Everything in the 2026 consumer AI market — chat assistants, copilots, agents — is a scaled, aligned descendant of exactly the decoder-only autoregressive loop we covered today.

slides.title: Quick Check: Did the Idea Land?
slides.body:
## Five questions to test yourself

**1. What does 'autoregressive' mean in the context of GPT?**
_Hint: think about how each new word is produced._

**2. Why can't a decoder-only model 'peek ahead' at future tokens during generation?**
a) It would be too slow
b) There is no future yet — the model is creating it one token at a time (causal masking)
c) The hardware doesn't allow it
d) It violates the license

**3. The objective 'predict the next word' is narrow and only teaches grammar.**
True or False? Explain.

**4. Why did OpenAI initially withhold the full GPT-2 model, and name one criticism of that decision.**

**5. Scenario:** You need to translate legal documents from English to German, where input and output are clearly different sequences. Which of the three architectures is the classic best fit, and why?

---

### Answers
1. It **generates one token at a time, feeding each prediction back as input** to predict the next — looping until done. Each step predicts the next word from all previous words.
2. **(b)** — at generation time the future doesn't exist yet; the model builds it token by token, so causal (masked) attention only lets each position see the past.
3. **False** — predicting the next word across all human text secretly requires facts, arithmetic, logic, emotion, code, translation, and reasoning. Great prediction requires broad understanding; that's the whole GPT thesis.
4. They feared **mass-produced misinformation, spam, and impersonation** from such fluent generation. A valid criticism: withholding hurt open science/safety research (you can't study what you can't access) and/or that it was partly a publicity move that wouldn't stop determined bad actors.
5. **Encoder-decoder** — the encoder fully reads/understands the English source, and the decoder generates the different German target sequence; this 'one sequence in, a different sequence out' shape is the classic encoder-decoder use case.
slides.speakerNotes: Run question 3 as a discussion, not a quick answer — it's the most important idea of the day and worth re-cementing. For question 5, acknowledge that a giant 2026 decoder LLM can also translate beautifully; the point is understanding the CLASSIC right-tool mapping. Question 4 has no single right opinion — let them argue both sides, mirroring the real 2019 debate.
slides.marketConnection: Question 4's tension — release openly vs. hold back for safety — is a live, unresolved 2026 industry fight between open-weight and closed frontier labs.

keyTakeaways: GPT is the decoder-only branch of the Transformer: Generative Pre-trained Transformer, built to produce text, not just judge it.
Autoregressive generation means predicting the next token from all previous tokens, then appending and repeating, using causal (masked) attention that blocks the future.
'Predict the next word' is a deceptively universal objective: doing it well across all human text requires facts, logic, arithmetic, emotion, and reasoning — great prediction requires understanding.
GPT-1 (2018, 117M) quietly proved the generative pretrain/fine-tune recipe; GPT-2 (2019, 1.5B) could write fluent articles and showed the first signs of in-context learning.
GPT-2's 'too dangerous to release' controversy was the first public collision of AI capability and safety, seeding the alignment and open-vs-closed debates still raging in 2026.

Three architectures, three jobs: encoder-only understands, decoder-only generates, encoder-decoder transforms one sequence into another — and generation won the spotlight by being visible, open-ended, and accessed through plain conversation.
marketSnapshot2026: As of June 2026, the decoder-only generative line has become the public face of artificial intelligence. Every mainstream chat assistant and copilot — ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and a long tail of open-weight models — is a scaled, aligned descendant of the exact autoregressive next-token loop GPT pioneered. The 'universal interface' insight (one model, any task, just ask in plain language) has reorganized entire industries around the chat box as the front door to computing. The temperature and top-p dials introduced here are standard knobs in every LLM API. Meanwhile, the GPT-2 release drama has matured into one of the defining political fault lines of the field: open-weight labs argue for transparency and democratized access, while closed frontier labs argue that the most capable models must be gated for safety. The core questions GPT-2 forced into the open in 2019 — should we release this, and who decides — remain unresolved and fiercely contested. Generation, once the quiet runner-up to BERT's benchmark wins, turned out to be the path that captured both the imagination and the market.
homework: Two parts. (1) Reflection: write a short paragraph defending one side of the GPT-2 debate — was 'too dangerous to release' responsible caution or an overreaction? Then write one sentence steelmanning the opposite view. (2) Hands-on (optional): use any text-generation playground (a small GPT-2 demo, or a modern model's API with temperature exposed) and generate the same prompt at temperature 0.2, 0.8, and 1.4. Record how coherence and creativity trade off. You are directly feeling the sampling mechanism that powers every chatbot you use.
nextDayTeaser: Tomorrow we answer the question GPT-2 left hanging: what happens when you don't just make it 10x bigger, but 100x bigger? Enter GPT-3 — 175 billion parameters — and a shock nobody fully predicted: it could learn brand-new tasks from a few examples in the prompt, with zero training. We'll decode the scaling laws that turned 'just make it bigger' into a strategy, and find out exactly where that strategy starts to bend.
